meta.description: Atomic ingredient-level ontology for MindCraft adaptive tutoring. Each concept decomposes into 4-6 irreducible mental models with learning vectors, failure priors grounded in research, and card templates for geometric/algebraic/procedural learners. Bridges encode the exact cognitive transitions between concepts. v2.3 adds ACT Test 2 Q8-Q15 stable ingredients for threshold functions, chart reading, absolute-value inequalities, coordinate distance, and basic log conversion.
meta.architecture_role: Layer 1 of 5: canonical math knowledge and cross-cutting learning skills.

schema_policy.rationale: Concepts are stable mathematical/cognitive skills. Question archetypes are exam-specific patterns that often bridge multiple concepts. Keeping them as linked node types preserves nuance and avoids bloating concept objects.
schema_policy.linking_contract: Concepts expose ingredient IDs and allowed archetype references. Archetypes and question instances reference concept/ingredient IDs rather than duplicating canonical definitions.

design_assumptions: Learning failure is often a translation failure, not a pure content gap.
Question difficulty is driven by representation, hidden bridges, time pressure, and misconception traps, not just by the primary topic.
A useful tutor backend must preserve partial evidence: a student may know the final formula but miss the setup bridge.

primary_secondary_tertiary_effects.primary: More precise diagnosis of why a student missed a question.
primary_secondary_tertiary_effects.secondary: Better adaptive practice generation because the system can target archetypes and misconceptions, not only topics.
primary_secondary_tertiary_effects.tertiary: A richer student model that can later power visual learning simulations and world-model-style explanations.

counterintuitive_insights: The best ontology should not store everything. Separating concepts from archetypes increases power because the same archetype can bridge multiple concepts and the same concept can appear in many disguises.

The fastest remediation is sometimes not reteaching the primary concept. For example, a student missing a square-diagonal problem may need help detecting implicit structure, not Pythagorean theorem itself.

canonical_id_contract.principle: Every layer references the same canonical IDs; layers may add context but must not redefine core meanings.
immutability_rules: Never change an ID once used in student-event data; deprecate and alias instead.
Raw source question text is immutable; intelligence annotations are versioned separately.
Concept definitions are canonical; question archetypes reference them and may not silently redefine them.
If an archetype bridges concepts, store that bridge explicitly rather than forcing one primary topic to contain the full meaning.

scale_policy.how_to_add_new_concepts: Add only when the skill is stable across many questions/curricula, not just a one-off test pattern.
scale_policy.how_to_add_new_ingredients: Add when a student can know the parent concept but still reliably fail because this sub-skill is missing.
scale_policy.how_to_handle_ACT_patterns: Store ACT patterns as archetypes that reference concept and ingredient IDs. Do not duplicate concept explanations inside archetypes.
scale_policy.review_cadence: After every 50 newly annotated questions, review whether repeated unmodeled failures require a new ingredient or concept.

meta.update_note: AI-generated ACT Math Test 21-30 annotation batch added coverage counts and cross-layer package provenance.
meta.combinations_layer_note: Co-occurrence hyperedges over ingredient IDs that must co-fire in a problem but are not connected by comes_from or bridge edges. AI-authored priors; frequency_weight and co_failure_prior are empirical TODOs to be backfilled from real problem decompositions.

population_failure_prior.notes: Students mechanically apply algorithms without understanding what a fraction represents, causing systematic errors when problems appear in non-standard form.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Start with fraction bars and area models before any notation
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with the formal a/b notation and equivalence rules
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Start with the LCM algorithm for addition then build meaning backward

ingredients.label: Fraction as part of a whole
ingredients.description: A fraction a/b means a equal parts out of b total parts of one whole unit.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students treat numerator and denominator as two separate unrelated numbers rather than as a ratio describing parts of a single whole

card_templates.geometric: Draw a bar divided into b equal sections and shade a of them — that shaded region is the fraction
card_templates.algebraic: a/b = a divided by b; the fraction bar IS the division symbol
card_templates.procedural: Count total equal parts (denominator), count shaded parts (numerator), write shaded/total

ingredients.label: Equivalent fractions via scaling
ingredients.description: Multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number produces an equal fraction.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students add or subtract the same number from both top and bottom instead of multiplying, producing non-equivalent fractions

card_templates.geometric: Doubling the number of sections and the shaded parts keeps the same proportion of the bar shaded
card_templates.algebraic: (a times k)/(b times k) = a/b because k/k = 1, and multiplying by 1 preserves value
card_templates.procedural: To make equivalent fractions: pick a multiplier k, multiply top AND bottom by k — never add to both

ingredients.label: Addition requires common denominator
ingredients.description: Fractions can only be added or subtracted when they represent parts of the same-sized whole — they need a common denominator.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students add numerators and denominators separately: 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5 instead of the correct 5/6

card_templates.geometric: You cannot add thirds and halves directly — convert both to sixths first so the pieces are the same size
card_templates.algebraic: a/b + c/d = (ad + bc)/(bd); you must express both fractions over the same denominator before adding
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: find LCM of denominators. Step 2: convert both fractions to that denominator. Step 3: add numerators only, keep denominator

ingredients.label: Decimal as positional base-10 fraction
ingredients.description: Each decimal place represents a power of 1/10: tenths, hundredths, thousandths.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students order decimals by digit count not value: concluding 0.25 is greater than 0.7 because 25 is greater than 7

card_templates.geometric: On a number line from 0 to 1: 0.7 sits at the 7th mark of 10; 0.25 sits at the 25th mark of 100 — 0.7 is farther right
card_templates.algebraic: 0.7 = 7/10 = 70/100; 0.25 = 25/100; since 70 is greater than 25, 0.7 is greater than 0.25
card_templates.procedural: To compare decimals: pad with trailing zeros to equal length (0.70 vs 0.25), then compare digit by digit left to right

ingredients.label: Fraction converts to decimal via division
ingredients.description: Any fraction a/b equals the decimal produced by dividing a by b.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students write 3/4 as 0.34 or 3.4 by simply concatenating digits without performing the division

card_templates.geometric: 3/4 means 3 wholes shared equally among 4 people — each person gets 0.75 of a whole
card_templates.algebraic: a/b = a divided by b; perform long division of numerator by denominator to get the decimal
card_templates.procedural: Divide the top number by the bottom number using long division; the quotient is the decimal

population_failure_prior.notes: Students cross-multiply mechanically but cannot set up proportions correctly from word problems because they confuse which quantity belongs in the numerator.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Start with similar figures and scale factors visually
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with the formal a/b = c/d setup and cross multiplication derivation
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Start with unit rate calculation and scale from there

ingredients.label: Ratio is multiplicative not additive
ingredients.description: A ratio a:b says how many times larger one quantity is than another, not how much more one is than the other.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students interpret ratio additively: ratio 3:1 means A is 2 more than B, not that A is 3 times B

card_templates.geometric: Draw 3 blue squares next to 1 red square — the ratio 3:1 shows a multiplicative relationship that is visible
card_templates.algebraic: a:b = a/b; if a/b = 3 then a = 3b, meaning A is three times B not two more than B
card_templates.procedural: Write ratio as fraction, evaluate the division, interpret: 3/1 means for every 1 of B there are 3 of A

ingredients.label: Unit rate expresses ratio per one unit
ingredients.description: A unit rate expresses a ratio with denominator 1, making comparison and scaling straightforward.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students cannot identify which quantity goes in the denominator when setting up a unit rate from a word problem

card_templates.geometric: Speed is distance per one hour — draw equal hourly intervals on a timeline to see the unit rate visually
card_templates.algebraic: Unit rate = total quantity / number of units; 150 miles / 3 hours = 50 miles per hour
card_templates.procedural: Divide both quantities so the denominator becomes exactly 1; the numerator is your unit rate

ingredients.label: Proportion sets two equal ratios
ingredients.description: Two ratios form a proportion when they are equal; setting them equal lets you solve for an unknown quantity.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students mix which values go in numerator vs denominator when translating a word problem into a proportion equation

card_templates.geometric: If 2 apples cost $3, draw the ratio apples/cost = 2/3; for 6 apples draw 6/x keeping the same unit order
card_templates.algebraic: Keep the same units in the same position on both sides: apples/cost = apples/cost
card_templates.procedural: Label both ratios with consistent units in numerator and denominator, set equal, solve for unknown

ingredients.label: Cross multiplication derives from fraction equality
ingredients.description: If a/b = c/d then ad = bc — cross multiplication is algebraically equivalent to multiplying both sides by bd.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students apply cross multiplication to non-proportion situations or forget it only applies when exactly two fractions are set equal

card_templates.geometric: Two rectangles with dimensions a by d and b by c have equal area when the ratios a:b and c:d are equal
card_templates.algebraic: a/b = c/d; multiply both sides by b to get a = bc/d; multiply both sides by d to get ad = bc
card_templates.procedural: Write the proportion, multiply diagonally in both directions, set those products equal, solve for unknown

population_failure_prior.notes: Students memorize PEMDAS as a strict left-to-right sequence, missing that multiplication and division are co-equal and must be done left to right among themselves.

ingredients.label: Operations have fixed priority levels
ingredients.description: Mathematical operations must be evaluated in fixed priority order: groupings first, then exponents, then multiplication and division, then addition and subtraction.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students evaluate strictly left-to-right regardless of operation type, computing 2 + 3 times 4 as 20 instead of 14

card_templates.geometric: Draw an expression tree — operations closer to the leaves must be evaluated before operations closer to the root
card_templates.algebraic: Think of precedence as binding strength: multiplication binds 3 and 4 more tightly than addition binds 2 and the product
card_templates.procedural: Circle all exponents first, then circle all multiplication and division groups, evaluate circled groups before moving to addition

positive: Student uses this ingredient correctly in a new context.

ingredients.label: Parentheses force evaluation first
ingredients.description: Any expression inside parentheses must be fully evaluated before it interacts with anything outside the parentheses.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students distribute into parentheses before evaluating inside them: 2(3+4) computed as 6+4 = 10 instead of 2 times 7 = 14

card_templates.geometric: Parentheses are a wall — everything inside must be fully resolved and collapsed to a single number before the wall comes down
card_templates.algebraic: 2(3+4) = 2 times 7 = 14; the parentheses collapse to a single value before multiplication
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: find innermost parentheses. Step 2: evaluate everything inside completely. Step 3: replace parentheses with that result

ingredients.label: Equal-precedence operations go left to right
ingredients.description: When two operations have equal precedence — both multiplication and division, or both addition and subtraction — evaluate from left to right.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute 12 divided by 4 times 3 as 12 divided by 12 = 1 instead of 3 times 3 = 9 because they multiply 4 and 3 first

card_templates.geometric: Read left to right: 12 divided by 4 is the first division or multiplication you encounter, so do it first: result is 3 times 3 = 9
card_templates.algebraic: Multiplication and division are co-equal; process whichever of the two appears first when reading left to right
card_templates.procedural: Scan left to right for multiplication or division, execute the first one found, replace with result, then scan again

ingredients.label: Exponent applies only to its immediate base
ingredients.description: An exponent applies only to its immediate base, not to a coefficient in front of it, unless parentheses explicitly group them together.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute 2x squared as (2x) squared when x=3, getting 36 instead of the correct 2 times 9 = 18

card_templates.geometric: The exponent is attached to x only — it belongs to x, not to the coefficient 2 in front of x
card_templates.algebraic: 2x squared = 2 times (x squared); the exponent applies only to x; (2x) squared = 4x squared is a completely different expression
card_templates.procedural: When substituting a value: replace x with the value in parentheses, compute x squared first, then multiply by the coefficient

population_failure_prior.notes: The equals-sign misconception — treating it as a compute command rather than a balance assertion — underlies a large proportion of algebraic errors across all subsequent topics.

ingredients.label: Equals sign means balance not compute
ingredients.description: The equals sign asserts that expressions on both sides have identical value; it is not a command to calculate.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students chain computations through equals signs: writing 5+3=8+2=10, using equals as meaning the answer so far is

card_templates.geometric: Imagine a balance scale — the equals sign is the fulcrum; both sides must weigh exactly the same
card_templates.algebraic: The symbol = is a statement of equality between two expressions, not an instruction to perform an operation
card_templates.procedural: Before writing an equals sign, ask: are both sides genuinely equal right now? The equals sign is a claim you must verify

ingredients.label: Inverse operations undo each other
ingredients.description: Addition and subtraction are inverses of each other; multiplication and division are inverses — applying an inverse undoes the original operation.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students subtract instead of dividing when undoing multiplication: solving 3x = 12 by writing x = 12 minus 3 = 9

card_templates.geometric: Addition moves right on a number line; subtraction moves left back to the start — they perfectly undo each other
card_templates.algebraic: If x + 5 = 12, subtract 5 from both sides: x + 5 minus 5 = 12 minus 5, giving x = 7
card_templates.procedural: Identify what operation is applied to x, then apply the exact opposite operation to both sides to undo it

ingredients.label: Same operation to both sides preserves equality
ingredients.description: An equation remains true if you add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides by the same nonzero value.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students apply an operation to only one side of the equation, breaking the balance without realizing it

card_templates.geometric: If you remove 3 kg from the left pan of a balance scale, you must remove exactly 3 kg from the right pan too
card_templates.algebraic: If a = b then a + c = b + c for any c; adding the same quantity to equal quantities preserves the equality
card_templates.procedural: Write the operation you want to apply on a new line, explicitly show it applied to both sides before simplifying

ingredients.label: Goal is variable alone with coefficient one
ingredients.description: Solving an equation means applying operations to get the variable by itself with a coefficient of 1 on one side of the equals sign.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students stop at 2x = 8 without dividing by the coefficient, leaving the variable with a coefficient other than 1

card_templates.geometric: Picture the variable trapped behind walls of operations — your goal is to demolish each wall layer by layer
card_templates.algebraic: The solved form is x = a number; keep applying inverse operations until that exact form is reached
card_templates.procedural: Undo operations in reverse order of PEMDAS: first undo addition and subtraction, then undo multiplication and division

ingredients.label: Substitute answer back to verify
ingredients.description: A solution is correct if substituting it back into the original equation makes both sides equal.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students skip verification entirely, missing sign errors or arithmetic mistakes that substitution would immediately catch

card_templates.geometric: Plug your answer back into the scale — if it tips to one side, your answer is wrong
card_templates.algebraic: If x = 4 is claimed, check: does 3(4) + 2 equal 14? Both sides must evaluate to the same number
card_templates.procedural: Replace every x with your answer, evaluate both sides independently, confirm they produce the same value

population_failure_prior.notes: Students calculate slope correctly but cannot interpret it as a rate of change with units, causing systematic failures on contextual word problems.

ingredients.label: Slope is rate of change with units
ingredients.description: Slope measures how much y changes for every 1 unit increase in x — it is a rate of change, not merely a steepness number.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute slope correctly but state it as a bare number without units or meaning: slope is 3, not y increases by 3 for every 1 unit increase in x

card_templates.geometric: On a graph count rise over run — for every step right, how many steps up? That ratio with its units is the slope
card_templates.algebraic: m = delta-y / delta-x = (y2 minus y1) / (x2 minus x1); always attach units such as dollars per hour
card_templates.procedural: Pick any two points, subtract y-values, subtract x-values in same order, divide — then write the units as output-units per input-unit

ingredients.label: Y-intercept is the value when x equals zero
ingredients.description: The y-intercept (b in y = mx + b) is the value of y when x = 0 — it represents the starting or initial value in context.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students think b is just a vertical shift with no real-world meaning and cannot identify the y-intercept from a word problem description

card_templates.geometric: Where does the line cross the vertical axis? That crossing point's y-coordinate is the y-intercept
card_templates.algebraic: Set x = 0 in any form: y = m(0) + b = b; the y-intercept is simply the output when input is zero
card_templates.procedural: Substitute x = 0 into the equation and solve for y — that y value is your y-intercept

ingredients.label: y = mx + b shows slope and intercept directly
ingredients.description: In slope-intercept form, m is readable as the slope and b is readable as the y-intercept directly from the equation.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students cannot identify m and b when the equation is not already in slope-intercept form: from 2y = 6x + 4 they say slope is 6

card_templates.geometric: Graph y = 2x + 3: start at the point (0, 3) on the y-axis, then go right 1 and up 2 repeatedly to draw the line
card_templates.algebraic: First isolate y completely; the coefficient attached to x is m, and the standalone constant is b
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: isolate y by dividing everything by y's coefficient. Step 2: identify the number next to x as m, the constant as b

ingredients.label: Two distinct points uniquely define a line
ingredients.description: Any two distinct points define exactly one straight line, allowing you to find the equation from any two coordinate pairs.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students try to determine slope from a single point alone or confuse which point goes in which position of the slope formula

card_templates.geometric: Plot two points on graph paper and place a ruler through them — there is exactly one straight line that passes through both
card_templates.algebraic: Calculate m = (y2 minus y1)/(x2 minus x1), then substitute into y minus y1 = m(x minus x1) and simplify
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: calculate slope from two points. Step 2: substitute slope and one point into y = mx + b. Step 3: solve for b

ingredients.label: Parallel share slope, perpendicular slopes are negative reciprocals
ingredients.description: Parallel lines have equal slopes; perpendicular lines have slopes whose product is negative one.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students write the perpendicular slope as just the negative of a slope, forgetting to also take the reciprocal: writing minus 2 as perpendicular to 2 instead of minus 1/2

card_templates.geometric: Draw two lines crossing at 90 degrees — if one rises 2 for every 1 right, the perpendicular falls 1 for every 2 right
card_templates.algebraic: If m1 times m2 = negative 1, lines are perpendicular; find perpendicular slope as m2 = negative 1 divided by m1
card_templates.procedural: For perpendicular slope: take the original slope, flip the fraction to get its reciprocal, then change the sign

population_failure_prior.notes: Students treat f(x) as f times x, producing cascading errors across every subsequent function topic.

ingredients.label: Function assigns exactly one output per input
ingredients.description: A function is a rule that assigns exactly one output to every valid input — no input can produce two different outputs.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students cannot recognize when a relation is not a function because they do not check whether any input maps to multiple outputs

card_templates.geometric: Draw a machine with one input slot and one output slot — each unique input must produce exactly one output to qualify as a function
card_templates.algebraic: f is a function if for every x in its domain, there exists exactly one y satisfying y = f(x)
card_templates.procedural: Vertical line test: if any vertical line crosses the graph more than once, the relation is not a function

ingredients.label: f(x) denotes output value not f times x
ingredients.description: f(x) is notation meaning the output of function f when the input is x — it is not multiplication of f and x.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute f(3) for f(x) = 2x + 1 as f multiplied by 3 giving an undefined product instead of 2(3) + 1 = 7

card_templates.geometric: f(3) means feed the number 3 into the machine named f and read what comes out
card_templates.algebraic: f(x) = 2x + 1; to compute f(3) substitute 3 everywhere x appears: 2(3) + 1 = 7
card_templates.procedural: Replace every occurrence of x in the rule with the given input value in parentheses, then evaluate the expression

ingredients.label: Domain is valid inputs, range is resulting outputs
ingredients.description: The domain is all inputs for which the function is defined; the range is the set of all resulting output values.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students confuse domain and range or cannot identify domain restrictions caused by division by zero or square roots of negatives

card_templates.geometric: On a graph: domain is all x-values where the curve has a point; range is all y-values where the curve has a point
card_templates.algebraic: For domain: find x-values causing division by zero or even root of a negative number — exclude those x-values
card_templates.procedural: For f(x) = 1/(x-2): set denominator not equal to zero, so x cannot equal 2; domain is all real numbers except x = 2

ingredients.label: Composition chains functions sequentially
ingredients.description: f(g(x)) means apply g first using x as input, then use g's output as the input to f.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute f(g(x)) as f(x) times g(x) or reverse the order by applying f before g

card_templates.geometric: Two machines in a pipeline: x enters g first, g's output then enters f, and f's output is the final result f(g(x))
card_templates.algebraic: f(g(x)): substitute g(x) in place of x wherever x appears in f; if f(x) = x+1 and g(x) = 2x then f(g(x)) = 2x+1
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: evaluate inner function g at x. Step 2: use that result as the new input to f. Step 3: simplify

ingredients.label: Inverse exists only for one-to-one functions
ingredients.description: A function has an inverse only if each output comes from exactly one input; the inverse swaps all input-output pairs.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students attempt to find inverses of non-one-to-one functions like x squared without first restricting the domain

card_templates.geometric: Horizontal line test: if any horizontal line crosses the graph more than once, the function has no inverse without restricting its domain
card_templates.algebraic: To find the inverse: swap x and y in the equation, then solve for y; the result is f-inverse(x)
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: replace f(x) with y. Step 2: swap x and y throughout. Step 3: solve for y. Step 4: write as f-inverse(x)

ingredients.label: Function rule changes after a threshold
ingredients.description: Some word-problem functions have a fixed base amount up to a threshold and a variable charge only for the amount beyond that threshold.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students multiply the rate by total input instead of only by the amount beyond the threshold, e.g. using 0.25m instead of 50 + 0.25(m - 1000).

card_templates.geometric: Draw a timeline: the first 1000 minutes are covered by the base fee; only the minutes to the right of 1000 get charged extra.
card_templates.algebraic: For m > 1000, extra minutes = m - 1000, so cost = base fee + rate(extra minutes).
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify the included amount. Step 2: subtract it from the total. Step 3: multiply only the extra part by the extra rate. Step 4: add the base fee.

population_failure_prior.notes: Students know SOHCAHTOA as a mnemonic but cannot correctly identify opposite and adjacent sides relative to a chosen angle, making any ratio setup fail.

ingredients.label: a squared plus b squared equals c squared
ingredients.description: In a right triangle, the sum of squares of the two legs equals the square of the hypotenuse.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students apply the Pythagorean theorem to non-right triangles or add the sides without squaring first

card_templates.geometric: Draw squares on each side of a right triangle — the combined area of the two smaller squares equals the area of the largest square
card_templates.algebraic: c squared = a squared + b squared; when solving for a leg: a squared = c squared minus b squared
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify the hypotenuse as the side opposite the right angle. Step 2: square all known sides. Step 3: solve algebraically for the unknown

ingredients.label: Side names depend on the reference angle
ingredients.description: Opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse are defined relative to a specific chosen angle — they change when you choose a different angle.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students label opposite and adjacent once and treat those labels as fixed properties of the triangle regardless of which angle is being used

card_templates.geometric: Point to angle theta: the side directly across the triangle from theta is opposite; the side touching theta but not the hypotenuse is adjacent
card_templates.algebraic: The labeling is relative to theta: switching which angle you designate as theta completely swaps opposite and adjacent
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: mark your chosen angle theta. Step 2: identify the side not touching theta as opposite. Step 3: the remaining non-hypotenuse side is adjacent

ingredients.label: Trig ratios are specific side ratios
ingredients.description: sin(theta) = opposite over hypotenuse, cos(theta) = adjacent over hypotenuse, tan(theta) = opposite over adjacent.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students use the wrong trig ratio because they confused opposite and adjacent, or they flip the numerator and denominator

card_templates.geometric: Label the three sides O, A, H relative to your chosen angle directly on the triangle, then read off which sides your ratio needs
card_templates.algebraic: sin = O/H, cos = A/H, tan = O/A; SOHCAHTOA gives the order of letters in each ratio
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: label O, A, H on the diagram. Step 2: identify which ratio connects your known and unknown sides. Step 3: write the equation and solve

ingredients.label: Special triangles have known exact side ratios
ingredients.description: 30-60-90 triangles have sides in ratio 1 to root-3 to 2; 45-45-90 triangles have sides in ratio 1 to 1 to root-2.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students place root-3 on the wrong side in a 30-60-90 triangle, putting it opposite 30 degrees instead of opposite 60 degrees

card_templates.geometric: Draw a 30-60-90 triangle and label: short leg opposite 30 degrees is 1, long leg opposite 60 degrees is root-3, hypotenuse is 2
card_templates.algebraic: If hypotenuse = 2k in a 30-60-90, then the short leg = k and the long leg = k times root-3
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify the angle measures. Step 2: find the shortest side opposite the smallest angle. Step 3: scale the known ratios using that side as the unit

ingredients.label: Distance between coordinate points uses a right triangle
ingredients.description: The distance between two coordinate points is found by forming a right triangle from horizontal and vertical changes and applying the Pythagorean theorem.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students subtract coordinates inconsistently or add the raw changes instead of squaring horizontal and vertical differences.

card_templates.geometric: Plot both points, draw the horizontal and vertical legs between them, then the segment connecting the points is the hypotenuse.
card_templates.algebraic: Distance = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2); this is the Pythagorean theorem on coordinate changes.
card_templates.procedural: Subtract x-values, subtract y-values, square both changes, add, and take the square root.

population_failure_prior.notes: Only 3 percent of students spontaneously use the radius to conceptualize radian angles; most treat trig as purely a triangle tool and fail completely on unit circle problems or circular motion applications.

ingredients.label: Angle measures an amount of rotation
ingredients.description: An angle is not just a shape between two lines but a measure of how far you rotate from a starting direction.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students believe angles can only be between 0 and 180 degrees and cannot conceptualize angles like 270 degrees or negative angles

card_templates.geometric: Imagine a clock hand starting at the 3 o'clock position (positive x-axis) and rotating counterclockwise — the angle measures exactly that rotation
card_templates.algebraic: Angles can be any real number: positive means counterclockwise, negative means clockwise, above 360 means multiple full rotations
card_templates.procedural: To visualize any angle: start at the positive x-axis and rotate counterclockwise by that many degrees to find the terminal side

ingredients.label: Unit circle point is (cos theta, sin theta)
ingredients.description: On a circle of radius 1, the point reached after rotating by angle theta has x-coordinate equal to cos(theta) and y-coordinate equal to sin(theta).
ingredients.failure_mode: Students know sin and cos as triangle ratios but cannot locate them on the unit circle or apply them to angles beyond 90 degrees

card_templates.geometric: Draw a unit circle, pick a point on it, drop a perpendicular to the x-axis — the horizontal distance to the y-axis is cos(theta), the vertical height is sin(theta)
card_templates.algebraic: The unit circle equation is x squared + y squared = 1; substituting x = cos(theta) and y = sin(theta) gives the Pythagorean trig identity
card_templates.procedural: To find sin and cos of any angle: locate the angle on the unit circle, read the y-coordinate for sin and the x-coordinate for cos

ingredients.label: sin(theta) is the vertical projection of the radius
ingredients.description: sin(theta) represents how high the endpoint of a unit radius sits above the center — it is literally a vertical distance.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students know sin as opposite over hypotenuse but cannot connect it to the vertical component of a rotating vector, causing failure on circular motion problems like y = 30 + 18 sin(theta)

card_templates.geometric: Draw a radius at angle theta on the unit circle and drop a vertical line down to the x-axis — that vertical leg is exactly sin(theta)
card_templates.algebraic: If the rotating arm has length r and angle theta, its vertical component is r times sin(theta); for r = 1 this simplifies to sin(theta)
card_templates.procedural: For any rotating arm of length r: vertical height above center = r times sin(angle of rotation)

ingredients.description: cos(theta) represents how far the endpoint of a unit radius sits horizontally from the center.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students swap sin and cos in applied problems, using cos for vertical height and sin for horizontal position

card_templates.geometric: Draw a radius at angle theta and drop a horizontal line to the y-axis — that horizontal segment is exactly cos(theta)
card_templates.algebraic: If rotating arm has length r at angle theta, its horizontal component is r times cos(theta); cos gives the x-direction distance
card_templates.procedural: For left-right position of a point on a rotating arm of length r: horizontal position = r times cos(angle)

ingredients.label: Reference angle maps any angle to first quadrant
ingredients.description: The reference angle is the acute angle between a terminal side and the nearest x-axis, used to compute trig values in any quadrant.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute trig values of angles like 150 or 225 degrees from scratch instead of using the reference angle and adjusting only the sign

card_templates.geometric: Draw the angle on the unit circle — the reference angle is the smallest angle from the terminal side down to the nearest x-axis
card_templates.algebraic: sin(150 degrees) = sin(180 minus 150) = sin(30 degrees) = 0.5; the sign adjusts based on quadrant
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: find reference angle as distance to nearest x-axis. Step 2: evaluate trig at reference angle. Step 3: apply correct quadrant sign using ASTC

ingredients.label: sin squared plus cos squared always equals one
ingredients.description: Because sin and cos are the y and x coordinates of a point on the unit circle, they always satisfy the Pythagorean relationship.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students treat this as an isolated memorized formula rather than recognizing it as the Pythagorean theorem applied directly to the unit circle

card_templates.geometric: On the unit circle, sin and cos are the two legs of a right triangle with hypotenuse 1; Pythagoras gives sin squared + cos squared = 1
card_templates.algebraic: This is just x squared + y squared = 1 (the unit circle equation) rewritten with x = cos(theta) and y = sin(theta)
card_templates.procedural: Use this to find sin when cos is known or vice versa: if cos(theta) = 0.6 then sin squared = 1 minus 0.36 = 0.64 so sin(theta) = 0.8

population_failure_prior.notes: Students conflate the limit of a function with its value at the point, making the need for limits — and therefore the entire concept of derivatives — impossible to understand.

ingredients.label: Limit describes approach not arrival
ingredients.description: The limit of f(x) as x approaches a describes what f(x) gets close to — it does not require f(a) to exist.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students evaluate limits by plugging in the limit point even when the function is undefined there, entirely missing the conceptual purpose

card_templates.geometric: Graph a function with a hole at x = 2 — the limit is the y-value both sides approach, even though no point exists at x = 2
card_templates.algebraic: The limit as x approaches 2 of (x squared minus 4)/(x minus 2) equals 4, even though plugging in x = 2 gives 0/0 in the original
card_templates.procedural: Try direct substitution first; if you get 0/0, factor and cancel the troublesome term, then substitute into the simplified expression

ingredients.label: Limit and function value are independent
ingredients.description: The limit as x approaches a can exist even when f(a) is undefined, and can differ from f(a) even when f(a) is defined.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students assume the limit always equals the function value and cannot explain why a limit existing is different from f(a) existing

card_templates.geometric: Draw a graph with a hole at x = 2 where the limit is 3, but a filled dot at (2, 5) — the limit is 3 and the function value is 5; they are independent
card_templates.algebraic: f(x) = (x squared minus 4)/(x minus 2) has no value at x = 2, but its limit as x approaches 2 equals 4 — these are completely separate facts
card_templates.procedural: Always compute the limit and the function value as two separate steps; then check whether they agree to determine continuity

ingredients.label: Left and right limits must match for limit to exist
ingredients.description: The two-sided limit exists only when the function approaches the same value from both the left and the right.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students declare a limit exists based on checking only one side, missing jump discontinuities where the two sides disagree

card_templates.geometric: Trace the graph approaching x = a from the left and record that y-value; then trace from the right and record; if they match, the limit exists
card_templates.algebraic: The limit exists if and only if the left-hand limit equals the right-hand limit at that point
card_templates.procedural: Evaluate the function at values slightly less than a to get the left limit; at values slightly greater to get the right limit; compare

ingredients.label: Continuity requires three simultaneous conditions
ingredients.description: A function is continuous at x = a if: f(a) is defined, the limit exists, and the limit equals f(a).
ingredients.failure_mode: Students declare continuity based on the graph looking smooth, missing removable discontinuities that are invisible at normal scale

card_templates.geometric: Continuous means you can draw it without lifting your pencil — any hole, jump, or vertical asymptote breaks continuity
card_templates.algebraic: Three conditions must all hold simultaneously: f(a) exists, the limit exists, and they are equal
card_templates.procedural: Check condition 1: is f(a) defined? Condition 2: does the limit exist? Condition 3: are they equal? All three must pass

ingredients.label: Derivative is a specific kind of limit
ingredients.description: The derivative f-prime(a) is the limit as h approaches zero of [f(a+h) minus f(a)] divided by h — the limit of a shrinking average slope.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students memorize derivative rules without knowing they come from this limit definition, making it impossible to understand why the rules work or derive new ones

card_templates.geometric: Draw two points on a curve and connect them with a straight line (secant); as the second point slides toward the first, that line becomes the tangent
card_templates.algebraic: The difference quotient [f(a+h) minus f(a)] / h is the slope between two points; taking the limit as h approaches zero gives the instantaneous slope
card_templates.procedural: Substitute f(a+h) and f(a) into the difference quotient, expand and simplify, cancel all remaining h terms, then take the limit as h approaches zero

population_failure_prior.notes: Chain rule errors account for the majority of derivative mistakes; students fail because recognizing composite function structure requires seeing the inner and outer functions simultaneously.

ingredients.label: Derivative is a slope function not a number
ingredients.description: f-prime(x) gives the slope of the tangent line to f at every point x — it is itself a new function, not a single value.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute f-prime(x) and treat the result as a single slope value rather than recognizing it is a function that gives slope at any point

card_templates.geometric: Graph f(x) = x squared; at every point draw the tangent line — f-prime(x) = 2x is the formula describing each tangent slope
card_templates.algebraic: If f(x) = x squared then f-prime(x) = 2x; evaluating f-prime(3) = 6 gives the slope specifically at the point x = 3
card_templates.procedural: Differentiate f(x) to produce f-prime(x); plug in a specific x-value only when you want the slope at that one particular point

ingredients.label: Power rule brings exponent down and reduces it
ingredients.description: The derivative of x to the n is n times x to the (n minus 1) — bring the exponent down as coefficient, reduce exponent by 1.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students forget to reduce the exponent: writing the derivative of x cubed as 3x cubed instead of 3x squared

card_templates.geometric: Verify: the slope of x cubed at x = 2 should be 3 times 4 = 12; check that the tangent line at (2, 8) actually has slope 12
card_templates.algebraic: d/dx of x to the n = n times x to the (n minus 1); d/dx of x cubed = 3 times x squared (bring 3 down, reduce exponent from 3 to 2)
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify exponent n. Step 2: write n as the new coefficient. Step 3: rewrite x with exponent n minus 1

ingredients.label: Chain rule multiplies outer and inner derivatives
ingredients.description: For f(g(x)), the derivative is f-prime(g(x)) times g-prime(x) — differentiate the outer function leaving inner intact, then multiply by inner's derivative.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students differentiate only the outer function or only the inner function, forgetting to multiply by the derivative of the inner function entirely

card_templates.geometric: Two gears connected: outer gear speed times inner gear speed gives the total rate at which the final output changes
card_templates.algebraic: d/dx of sin(x squared) = cos(x squared) times 2x; outer derivative evaluated at inner, then multiplied by inner's derivative
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify the outer and inner functions. Step 2: differentiate outer leaving inner unchanged. Step 3: multiply the result by the derivative of the inner

ingredients.label: Products and quotients use special derivative rules
ingredients.description: The derivative of f times g equals f-prime times g plus f times g-prime; the derivative of f over g equals (f-prime times g minus f times g-prime) divided by g squared.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students differentiate products as f-prime times g-prime, incorrectly treating differentiation as distributing over multiplication

card_templates.geometric: Rectangle with sides f and g both changing: total area rate = rate of f times g plus f times rate of g — that is the product rule
card_templates.algebraic: Product rule: (fg)' = f'g + fg'; this is NOT the same as f'g' — differentiation does not distribute over multiplication
card_templates.procedural: Label the two functions u and v; product: u'v + uv'; quotient: (u'v minus uv') divided by v squared (low-d-high minus high-d-low over low squared)

ingredients.label: Derivative has units and real-world meaning
ingredients.description: In applied problems f-prime(x) carries units equal to output units divided by input units — it represents the rate of change in context.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute derivatives correctly but cannot interpret what f-prime(3) = 5 means in a word problem context

card_templates.geometric: If the position graph curves upward, velocity which is its derivative is positive and increasing — the tangent slope IS the speed at each moment
card_templates.algebraic: If P(t) is position in meters then P-prime(t) has units meters per second — attach output-units per input-unit to every derivative
card_templates.procedural: Units of f-prime(x) = units of f(x) divided by units of x; interpret as how much f changes per 1 unit increase in x at the specific point

population_failure_prior.notes: Students apply equation-solving steps correctly but forget the flip rule when multiplying or dividing by a negative — a single-step rule with outsized failure rate.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Start by graphing on a number line — show the solution as a region not a single point
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with the parallel to equations, then introduce the one exception (the flip)
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Start with a checklist: solve like an equation, check the sign of what you divided by, flip if negative

ingredients.label: Inequality solution is a range not a point
ingredients.description: Solving an inequality produces an infinite set of values — a region on the number line — not a single answer.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students write x = 3 instead of x greater than 3, treating the inequality as if it has one solution like an equation

card_templates.geometric: Draw a number line and shade everything to the right of 3 with an open circle — that entire shaded region is the solution, not just the point 3
card_templates.algebraic: x greater than 3 means any value like 3.1, 4, 100 all satisfy the inequality — there are infinitely many solutions
card_templates.procedural: After solving, verify by plugging in two values: one inside your solution region and one outside — both results should behave correctly

ingredients.label: Multiplying or dividing by negative flips the sign
ingredients.description: When you multiply or divide both sides of an inequality by a negative number, the inequality symbol must reverse direction.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students divide by a negative coefficient without flipping: solving negative-2x greater than 6 and writing x greater than negative-3 instead of x less than negative-3

card_templates.geometric: On a number line 2 is to the right of 1, meaning 2 is greater. Multiply both by negative-1: negative-2 is now to the LEFT of negative-1, so the relationship flipped
card_templates.algebraic: Start with 3 greater than 1. Multiply both sides by negative-1: negative-3 vs negative-1. Now negative-3 is less than negative-1 — the direction reversed
card_templates.procedural: At the step where you divide or multiply by a number: check its sign first. If negative, flip the inequality symbol before writing the next line

card_templates.geometric: Open circle means the boundary is a wall you cannot touch. Closed circle means you can stand on it. Draw both and compare
card_templates.algebraic: Strictly greater than or less than uses open circle. Greater than or equal to or less than or equal to uses filled circle
card_templates.procedural: Check whether the inequality includes equals. If yes, fill the circle. If no, leave it open

card_templates.procedural: Step 1: isolate x by applying the same operation to all three parts. Step 2: read the resulting interval. Step 3: check a value inside it satisfies both original conditions

ingredients.label: Absolute value greater-than splits into two outside intervals
ingredients.description: For |A| > k with k positive, the inside expression must be greater than k or less than negative k, producing two separate outside intervals.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students solve only A > k and forget A < -k, or incorrectly write the answer as a middle interval.

card_templates.geometric: On a number line, |A| > 11 means A is more than 11 units away from zero, so it lives outside the interval from -11 to 11.
card_templates.procedural: For absolute value greater than: split into two inequalities, use OR, and flip to the negative boundary on the second branch.

population_failure_prior.notes: Students can solve when a unique solution exists but break completely when the system has no solution or infinite solutions because they have no geometric intuition for what those cases mean.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Start by graphing two lines and asking where they cross
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with substitution method as the most direct algebraic approach
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Start with elimination as the most systematic step-by-step method

ingredients.label: Solution is where both lines cross
ingredients.description: The solution to a system of two equations is the point that satisfies both simultaneously — geometrically, where the two lines intersect.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students find an x-value by eliminating y but then forget to substitute back to find the y-value, reporting an incomplete solution

card_templates.geometric: Graph both lines on the same axes — the point where they cross is the one coordinate pair that makes both equations true at once
card_templates.algebraic: A solution is a pair (x, y) that satisfies both equations simultaneously — plug it into each equation and both must balance
card_templates.procedural: After finding x, always substitute back into either original equation to find y — both coordinates are required

ingredients.label: Substitution replaces one variable with an expression
ingredients.description: Solve one equation for one variable, then substitute that expression into the other equation to get one equation in one unknown.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students substitute the expression back into the same equation they used to solve it instead of the other equation, producing a tautology

card_templates.geometric: Think of it as naming one variable in terms of the other — then you only have one unknown left to deal with
card_templates.algebraic: If y = 2x + 1 from equation 1, substitute (2x + 1) in place of y in equation 2 — not back into equation 1
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: isolate one variable in one equation. Step 2: substitute that expression into the OTHER equation. Step 3: solve. Step 4: back-substitute to find second variable

ingredients.label: Elimination cancels one variable by adding equations
ingredients.description: Multiply equations to make the coefficients of one variable equal and opposite, then add to eliminate that variable.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students subtract equations when they should add, or forget to multiply the entire equation (not just one term) before adding

card_templates.geometric: Imagine two scales — adding them together combines all weights simultaneously, canceling anything that appears equally on both sides
card_templates.algebraic: To eliminate y: multiply equation 1 by the coefficient of y in equation 2, and equation 2 by the negative of y's coefficient in equation 1
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: choose which variable to eliminate. Step 2: multiply each entire equation to make those coefficients opposites. Step 3: add equations. Step 4: solve. Step 5: back-substitute

ingredients.label: Parallel lines have no solution, identical lines have infinite
ingredients.description: When elimination produces a false statement like 0 = 5, no solution exists (parallel lines); when it produces 0 = 0, infinite solutions exist (same line).
ingredients.failure_mode: Students reach 0 = 5 mid-elimination and think they made an arithmetic error, erasing correct work instead of recognizing the system has no solution

card_templates.geometric: Graph the two lines — if they are parallel they never cross (no solution); if they are the same line every point on it is a solution (infinite)
card_templates.algebraic: 0 = 5 is always false — no values of x and y can make this true, confirming no intersection exists
card_templates.procedural: When elimination gives 0 = constant: if constant is nonzero, write no solution; if constant is zero, write infinite solutions and express as a line

population_failure_prior.notes: Negative exponents are the single most-failed exponent topic — students either ignore the negative or move the wrong factor to the denominator.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Show the pattern by expanding: x cubed times x squared written out as individual x factors

ingredients.label: Same base multiplication adds exponents
ingredients.description: When multiplying two powers with the same base, add the exponents: x to the a times x to the b equals x to the (a+b).
ingredients.failure_mode: Students multiply the exponents instead of adding: x squared times x cubed becomes x to the sixth instead of x to the fifth

card_templates.geometric: Write out x squared as x times x, and x cubed as x times x times x — combined you have five x factors, confirming x to the fifth
card_templates.algebraic: x to the a times x to the b = x to the (a+b); you are counting total factors of x so you add the counts
card_templates.procedural: Check the bases are identical. If yes, keep the base and add the exponents. Never multiply the exponents here

card_templates.geometric: Write x to the fifth over x squared as five x factors over two x factors — cancel two from top and bottom leaving three: x cubed
card_templates.algebraic: x to the a divided by x to the b = x to the (a minus b); top exponent minus bottom exponent in that order
card_templates.procedural: Identify top exponent and bottom exponent. Subtract bottom from top. Keep the same base. Never divide the exponents

ingredients.label: Power of a power multiplies exponents
ingredients.description: When raising a power to another power, multiply the exponents: (x to the a) to the b equals x to the (a times b).
ingredients.failure_mode: Students add exponents instead of multiplying: (x squared) cubed becomes x to the fifth instead of x to the sixth

card_templates.geometric: Expand (x squared) cubed as x squared times x squared times x squared — that is three groups of two x factors = six total: x to the sixth
card_templates.algebraic: (x to the a) to the b = x to the (a times b); you are applying the product rule b times in a row, so you multiply
card_templates.procedural: When you see a power inside parentheses raised to another power: multiply the two exponents, keep the base

ingredients.label: Negative exponent means reciprocal
ingredients.description: x to the negative n equals 1 divided by x to the n — a negative exponent moves the factor to the other side of the fraction bar.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students make the base negative: x to the negative-2 becomes negative x squared instead of 1 over x squared

card_templates.geometric: Show the pattern: x cubed, x squared, x to the one, x to the zero equals 1 — each step divides by x. One more step gives x to the negative-1 = 1/x
card_templates.algebraic: x to the negative-n = 1 over x to the n; the negative only affects position in the fraction not the sign of the number
card_templates.procedural: Spot any negative exponent. Move that factor across the fraction bar. Make the exponent positive. Never change the sign of the base

ingredients.label: Zero exponent is one, fractional exponent is a root
ingredients.description: Any nonzero number to the zero power equals 1. A fractional exponent x to the (p/q) means the q-th root of x to the p.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute x to the zero as zero, and x to the (1/2) as x over 2 instead of the square root of x

card_templates.geometric: x to the zero = x to the (n minus n) = x to the n over x to the n = 1 for any nonzero x — show this cancellation visually
card_templates.algebraic: x to the (1/2) = square root of x because squaring both sides gives x to the 1 = x. Similarly x to the (p/q) = q-th root of x to the p
card_templates.procedural: For x to the zero: write 1. For x to the (p/q): write as q-th root of (x to the p) then evaluate inside first, then take the root

population_failure_prior.notes: Students correctly add and subtract polynomials but fail at multiplication, either forgetting to distribute every term or misapplying the distributive property.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Use area model for polynomial multiplication — rows and columns of terms
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Use formal distributive property notation showing each pairing
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Use FOIL as a systematic checklist for binomials then generalize

ingredients.label: Only like terms can be combined
ingredients.description: Like terms have identical variable parts — the same variables raised to the same powers — and can be added or subtracted by combining their coefficients.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students combine unlike terms: adding 3x squared and 2x to get 5x squared or 5x instead of leaving them separate

card_templates.geometric: Think of x squared as apples and x as oranges — you can count apples together and oranges together but you cannot combine them
card_templates.algebraic: 3x squared + 2x squared = 5x squared because the variable part x squared is identical; 3x squared + 2x cannot be combined
card_templates.procedural: Sort terms into groups with identical variable parts. Add or subtract coefficients within each group. Leave different groups separate

ingredients.label: Every term must be multiplied by every other term
ingredients.description: When multiplying a monomial by a polynomial, the monomial multiplies each and every term inside — no term can be skipped.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students multiply the first term only: 3(x squared + 2x + 1) becomes 3x squared + 2x + 1 instead of 3x squared + 6x + 3

card_templates.geometric: Draw arrows from the 3 outside to each term inside: 3 times x squared, 3 times 2x, 3 times 1 — every term gets an arrow
card_templates.algebraic: 3(x squared + 2x + 1) = 3 times x squared + 3 times 2x + 3 times 1 = 3x squared + 6x + 3
card_templates.procedural: Count the terms inside the parentheses. You must write that many products. Cross off each term as you multiply it

ingredients.label: Binomial multiplication needs all four pairings
ingredients.description: Multiplying two binomials requires four products: First, Outer, Inner, Last — every term in the first binomial multiplied by every term in the second.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students multiply only the first terms and last terms: (x+2)(x+3) becomes x squared + 6 instead of x squared + 5x + 6

card_templates.geometric: Draw a 2 by 2 area model: top row is x and 2, left column is x and 3 — fill in all four cells then sum them
card_templates.algebraic: (x+2)(x+3) = x times x + x times 3 + 2 times x + 2 times 3 = x squared + 3x + 2x + 6 = x squared + 5x + 6
card_templates.procedural: Circle the four letter pairs F-O-I-L. Write each product on its own line. Combine like terms at the end. Verify you have four terms before combining

ingredients.label: Degree is the highest exponent sum in any term
ingredients.description: The degree of a polynomial is the highest total exponent across all variables in any single term.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students identify degree as the largest single exponent rather than the largest sum of exponents in a term: calling x squared y cubed degree 3 instead of degree 5

card_templates.geometric: Think of degree as total dimensionality — x squared y cubed lives in 5-dimensional space because the exponents sum to 5
card_templates.algebraic: For each term add all variable exponents together. The polynomial's degree is the largest of those sums
card_templates.procedural: Go term by term: sum the exponents in each term, record the sum, then take the maximum across all terms

ingredients.label: Factor out the greatest common monomial factor
ingredients.description: To factor a polynomial expression by GCF, remove the largest coefficient and variable powers shared by every term, then divide each term by that factor.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students factor out a number but not the common variables, or subtract exponents incorrectly when dividing monomials.

card_templates.geometric: Think of each term as a product of blocks; pull out only the blocks that every term has in common.
card_templates.algebraic: For 12x²y³z⁴ − 30xy² + 24x²y⁵z, the common monomial is 6xy²; divide each term by 6xy² to get the bracket.
card_templates.procedural: Find GCF of coefficients, then minimum exponent of each variable across all terms. That product is the GCF monomial.

population_failure_prior.notes: Students can factor when the leading coefficient is 1 but fail consistently when it is not — the ac method is the most-skipped skill in the entire algebra curriculum.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Use area model in reverse — fill in the known cells and work backward to find the dimensions
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Use the systematic factor pairs approach with product and sum conditions
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Use a decision tree: check for GCF first, then count terms, then apply the matching technique

ingredients.label: Always factor out GCF first
ingredients.description: Before any other factoring, identify the greatest common factor of all terms and factor it out — this simplifies every subsequent step.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students jump to other factoring techniques without extracting the GCF, leaving unnecessarily large numbers that make the remaining steps harder

card_templates.geometric: The GCF is the largest tile that fits evenly into every term — remove it and see what is left in each section
card_templates.algebraic: Find the largest number and highest power that divides every term: for 6x cubed + 4x squared the GCF is 2x squared
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: list all coefficients and find their GCF. Step 2: find the lowest exponent for each variable across all terms. Step 3: factor out that product. Step 4: verify by distributing back

ingredients.label: Quadratic factoring finds two numbers with specific product and sum
ingredients.description: For x squared + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b — those numbers are the constants in the factored form.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students find pairs that multiply to c but ignore the sum condition, or find pairs that add to b but forget to check the product

card_templates.geometric: Reverse the FOIL area model: you know the area is x squared + 5x + 6 — what dimensions of a rectangle produce that area? Try x+2 by x+3
card_templates.algebraic: For x squared + 5x + 6: need two numbers multiplying to 6 AND adding to 5. List factor pairs of 6: (1,6) sum 7, (2,3) sum 5. Answer: (x+2)(x+3)
card_templates.procedural: List all factor pairs of c systematically. For each pair check whether it sums to b. The first pair that passes both tests gives your factored form

ingredients.label: When leading coefficient is not one use the ac method
ingredients.description: For ax squared + bx + c with a not equal to 1: find two numbers multiplying to ac and adding to b, then split the middle term and factor by grouping.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students apply the simple factor pairs method to ax squared + bx + c with a not 1 and get wrong factors because they only look for pairs multiplying to c not to ac

card_templates.geometric: Use the area model with unknown dimensions — the total area is 2x squared + 7x + 3; the four cells must sum to this; fill in what you know and deduce the rest
card_templates.algebraic: For 2x squared + 7x + 3: ac = 6, need factors of 6 adding to 7: that is 1 and 6. Rewrite as 2x squared + x + 6x + 3, then factor by grouping
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: compute a times c. Step 2: find factor pairs of ac summing to b. Step 3: rewrite the middle term using those two numbers. Step 4: factor by grouping. Step 5: verify

ingredients.label: Difference of squares and perfect square trinomials have shortcuts
ingredients.description: a squared minus b squared factors as (a+b)(a-b); a perfect square trinomial factors as (a plus b) squared.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students do not recognize these patterns and apply the full ac method unnecessarily, or they factor a squared minus b squared as (a-b) squared

card_templates.geometric: Draw a square with area a squared, remove a square with area b squared from one corner — the remaining L-shaped region has area (a+b)(a-b)
card_templates.algebraic: x squared minus 9 = x squared minus 3 squared = (x+3)(x-3); the two factors are sum and difference of the square roots
card_templates.procedural: Check: are both terms perfect squares with a minus between them? If yes apply the shortcut directly rather than factoring from scratch

population_failure_prior.notes: Students correctly simplify individual radicals but fail when adding them — they add the radicands the way they add like terms, not understanding that the index not the radicand determines like terms.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Show radicals as side lengths of geometric figures to build intuition
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with the definition as the inverse of squaring and build from there
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Use prime factorization as the standard simplification algorithm

ingredients.label: Square root undoes squaring
ingredients.description: The square root of x is the number that when squared gives x — it is the inverse operation of squaring.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute the square root of 25 as 12.5 (dividing by 2) instead of 5, confusing the square root with halving

card_templates.geometric: The square root of 25 is the side length of a square with area 25 — draw the square and count: each side is 5
card_templates.algebraic: Square root of x = x to the power 1/2; (square root of x) squared = x because the operations cancel
card_templates.procedural: To find the square root of n: ask what number times itself equals n. For 36: 6 times 6 = 36, so square root of 36 = 6

ingredients.label: Simplify by pulling out perfect square factors
ingredients.description: Factor the radicand to find perfect square factors, then pull them outside the radical as their square roots.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students divide the radicand by 2 instead of factoring, or pull out factors that are not perfect squares

card_templates.geometric: Square root of 48: draw 48 as a rectangle of area 48, factor out the largest perfect square tile (16), leaving 3 inside
card_templates.algebraic: Square root of 48 = square root of (16 times 3) = square root of 16 times square root of 3 = 4 times square root of 3
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: find the largest perfect square that divides the radicand. Step 2: split the radical using that factor. Step 3: simplify the perfect square root. Step 4: leave the rest inside

ingredients.label: Add radicals only when the radicand is identical
ingredients.description: Radicals with the same number under the root sign are like terms and can be added by combining their coefficients.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students add the radicands: 2 times square root of 3 plus 3 times square root of 3 becomes 5 times square root of 6 instead of 5 times square root of 3

card_templates.geometric: Think of square root of 3 as a unit: 2 units + 3 units = 5 units, so 2 root-3 + 3 root-3 = 5 root-3
card_templates.algebraic: Only the coefficients add: 2 times square root of 3 + 3 times square root of 3 = (2+3) times square root of 3 = 5 times square root of 3
card_templates.procedural: First simplify each radical separately. Then check whether radicands match. If yes add coefficients. If no they cannot be combined

ingredients.label: Rationalize by multiplying by a clever form of one
ingredients.description: To eliminate a radical from a denominator, multiply numerator and denominator by the radical (or conjugate) — this produces an integer in the denominator.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students add the radical to the denominator instead of multiplying, or only multiply the denominator and not the numerator

card_templates.geometric: You are multiplying by 1 written cleverly — square root of 2 over square root of 2 equals 1, so the value does not change
card_templates.algebraic: 1 over square root of 2 = (1 times square root of 2) over (square root of 2 times square root of 2) = square root of 2 over 2
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: identify the radical in the denominator. Step 2: multiply top and bottom by that exact radical. Step 3: simplify the denominator. Step 4: simplify the numerator

population_failure_prior.notes: Students apply the quadratic formula correctly but cannot predict the nature of solutions from the discriminant, and cannot connect the algebraic solutions to the graph's x-intercepts.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Start by graphing parabolas and identifying solutions as x-intercepts; use the root-averaging trick for vertex
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Start with standard form and discriminant analysis before solving
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Start with factoring as method 1, quadratic formula as fallback method 2

ingredients.label: Solutions are x-intercepts of the parabola
ingredients.description: The solutions to ax squared + bx + c = 0 are the x-coordinates where the parabola y = ax squared + bx + c crosses the x-axis.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students solve quadratics algebraically without any connection to the graph, making it impossible to catch unreasonable answers

card_templates.geometric: Graph y = x squared minus 5x + 6 and read where it crosses the x-axis — those crossings at x=2 and x=3 are the solutions to x squared minus 5x + 6 = 0
card_templates.algebraic: Setting y = 0 in y = ax squared + bx + c gives the equation whose solutions are the x-intercepts
card_templates.procedural: After solving, check reasonableness on a rough sketch — your x-values should be where a parabola with that shape would plausibly cross the x-axis

ingredients.label: Vertex x-coordinate is the average of the roots
ingredients.description: Because a parabola is symmetric, the vertex sits exactly halfway between the two roots — average the x-intercepts to find it.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students use the formula x = negative b over 2a without understanding why it works, so they cannot verify it or adapt when the formula is unfamiliar

card_templates.geometric: Graph the parabola and draw a vertical line through the vertex — it is exactly the mirror line between the two x-intercepts, so average them to find it
card_templates.algebraic: If roots are r1 and r2 then vertex x = (r1 + r2) divided by 2; this is also negative b over 2a from Vieta's formulas
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: find both roots. Step 2: add them and divide by 2. Step 3: substitute that x into the original equation to find the vertex y-coordinate

ingredients.label: Factoring uses the zero product property
ingredients.description: If a product of factors equals zero then at least one factor must be zero — set each factor equal to zero and solve independently.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students factor correctly but then add the constants: (x-2)(x-3) = 0 gives x = 5 because they add 2 and 3 instead of setting each factor to zero

card_templates.geometric: If two lengths multiply to zero area, at least one length must be zero — the same logic applies to any two factors
card_templates.algebraic: Zero product property: if AB = 0 then A = 0 OR B = 0; set each factor independently equal to zero and solve
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: factor the quadratic completely. Step 2: write Factor-1 = 0 and Factor-2 = 0 as two separate equations. Step 3: solve each independently

card_templates.geometric: The formula gives x-coordinates of both intersections with the x-axis — plus gives one crossing, minus gives the other
card_templates.algebraic: Identify a, b, c from ax squared + bx + c carefully with signs: for 2x squared minus 5x + 3, a=2, b=negative 5, c=3
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: write a, b, c with their signs. Step 2: compute b squared minus 4ac. Step 3: take the square root. Step 4: apply plus and minus for two answers. Step 5: divide everything by 2a

ingredients.label: Discriminant predicts number and type of solutions

card_templates.geometric: Positive discriminant: parabola cuts x-axis at two points. Zero: it just touches the x-axis at one point. Negative: it floats above or below without touching
card_templates.algebraic: The discriminant is under the square root — negative means the square root is imaginary, so no real solutions exist

ingredients.label: Mean is the balance point of the data
ingredients.description: The mean is the sum of all values divided by the count — it is the single value that would balance the data if placed on a scale.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute the mean correctly but cannot explain what it represents or why outliers pull it far from the center of most values

card_templates.geometric: Plot the data on a number line and imagine a balance point — the mean is where the fulcrum would need to be so the data balances
card_templates.algebraic: Mean = sum of all values divided by number of values; one very large outlier increases the sum significantly and pulls the mean toward it
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: add all values. Step 2: divide by count of values. Step 3: check whether the result falls near most of the data or is pulled by an outlier

ingredients.label: Median is the middle value when ordered
ingredients.description: The median is the middle value when all data is arranged in order — it is not affected by extreme values at either end.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students find the median of an even-count dataset by picking one of the two middle values instead of averaging both

card_templates.geometric: Line up all values from smallest to largest — the median is the person standing exactly in the middle; with even count it is halfway between the two middle people
card_templates.algebraic: For odd count: median is value at position (n+1)/2. For even count: median is average of values at positions n/2 and (n/2)+1
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: sort all values from smallest to largest. Step 2: count n. Step 3: if odd, pick the middle. If even, average the two middle values

ingredients.label: Outliers pull the mean but not the median

card_templates.geometric: Histogram skewed right with one huge outlier: the mean is pulled far right while the median stays near the bulk of the data — draw both positions
card_templates.algebraic: Dataset: 1, 2, 3, 4, 100. Mean = 110/5 = 22. Median = 3. The mean of 22 does not describe any actual value in the data well

ingredients.label: Read categorical data from charts
ingredients.description: Pie charts, bar charts, and categorical displays encode quantities by sections or bars; solving requires extracting the correct category values before computing.

card_templates.geometric: Point to the named category first, read its percent or count, then only do arithmetic after the chart value is confirmed.
card_templates.algebraic: Translate chart value into a number: category votes = category percent × total votes.
card_templates.procedural: Circle the named category, write its percentage, convert to decimal if needed, then multiply by the total.

population_failure_prior.notes: Students confuse P(A and B) with P(A or B) and fail to recognize when events are independent versus dependent — the conditional probability concept breaks down completely without independence intuition.

learning_style_affinity.geometric_learner: Use Venn diagrams and area models to show probability as proportional area
learning_style_affinity.algebraic_learner: Use formal probability notation and the addition and multiplication rules
learning_style_affinity.procedural_learner: Use sample space enumeration and counting before introducing formulas

ingredients.label: Probability is favorable outcomes over total outcomes
ingredients.description: The probability of an event is the number of outcomes that produce the event divided by the total number of equally likely outcomes.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students count favorable outcomes but forget to count the total sample space, reporting a count rather than a ratio

card_templates.geometric: Draw all possible outcomes as equal-sized squares — the probability is the fraction of squares that satisfy your event
card_templates.algebraic: P(event) = number of favorable outcomes divided by total number of possible outcomes; both must be counted from the same sample space
card_templates.procedural: Step 1: list all possible outcomes. Step 2: identify which ones satisfy the event. Step 3: divide favorable count by total count

ingredients.label: P of not A equals one minus P of A
ingredients.description: The probability of an event not occurring equals one minus the probability that it does occur — all probabilities in a sample space sum to one.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students compute complement by subtracting from the favorable count rather than from 1, giving values outside the 0 to 1 range

card_templates.geometric: Draw the sample space as a rectangle with area 1 — shade the event; the complement is the unshaded portion and both must add to fill the whole rectangle
card_templates.algebraic: P(not A) = 1 minus P(A); since P(A) + P(not A) must equal 1 (all outcomes covered exactly once)
card_templates.procedural: When direct counting is hard: compute P(A), then subtract from 1 to get P(not A) — often much easier than counting the complement directly

ingredients.label: AND means intersection, OR means union
ingredients.description: P(A and B) is the probability both happen simultaneously; P(A or B) is the probability at least one of them happens.
ingredients.failure_mode: Students use the multiplication rule for OR problems and the addition rule for AND problems — they mix up which formula goes with which word

card_templates.geometric: Venn diagram: AND is the overlapping region in the middle; OR is everything covered by either circle including the overlap
card_templates.algebraic: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) minus P(A and B); you subtract the intersection to avoid counting it twice
card_templates.procedural: See the word AND: think overlap, use multiplication for independent events. See the word OR: think union, use addition minus overlap

ingredients.label: Conditional probability updates sample space
ingredients.description: P(A given B) is the probability of A occurring knowing that B has already occurred — it restricts the sample space to only outcomes where B is true.
